definition: The mix of demand vs. supply shocks is the fundamental determinant of stock-bond correlation sign. Demand shocks move output and inflation in the same direction, producing negative stock-bond price correlation under an optimizing central bank. Supply/inflation shocks move them in opposite directions, producing positive correlation. Derived structurally from the New Keynesian three-equation model.
evidence: 2022 episode: S&P 500 EPS grew ~4% yet index fell ~19%, entirely driven by discount rate repricing during supply-shock-dominated inflation surge
CPI prints became dominant single-day co-mover of both asset classes in 2022
Campbell, Sunderam & Viceira (2017) canonical empirical validation: inflation-consumption growth correlation sign determines bond hedging properties
NK three-equation model (IS, Phillips Curve, Taylor Rule) derives this structurally via impulse response sign restrictions
Campbell-Pflueger-Viceira (2020) framework endorsed by all 8 agents as highest-confidence theoretical foundation
6/7 flips to negative triggered by growth shocks across 13 documented post-1960 transitions
5/6 flips to positive triggered by inflation shocks with median lead time 2-4 months
Current ambiguous shock mix (residual supply-side inflation persistence + demand softening) explains why correlation regime remains unresolved
No agent contested the underlying mechanism; all debate concerns which shock type dominates going forward
open_questions: How do tariff shocks classify — demand or supply? Mixed shocks with ambiguous correlation implications
Does the framework oversimplify by collapsing inflation uncertainty, monetary regime, fiscal stance, and risk appetite into a single inflation regime variable?
Which shock type will dominate going forward given the current ambiguous mix of residual supply-side inflation persistence and demand softening?
How to decompose demand vs. supply shocks in real-time when components are mixed?
Does fiscal dominance alter the shock taxonomy by making the CB reaction function endogenous?
